documents.content: For senior backend engineer (L5/E5 equivalent) loops at FAANG-tier companies, expect 5 rounds in a single day: two coding rounds (one medium-hard algorithm, one practical/object-oriented design), one system design (45-60 minutes designing a real-world system at scale), one behavioral with a hiring manager, and one bar raiser or principal engineer round that combines technical depth with leadership probes. The bar at senior level isn't about whether you can solve a LeetCode hard — it's about communication, scope handling, and tradeoff reasoning. Candidates often fail not because they can't code, but because they don't ask clarifying questions, don't communicate their thought process, or can't articulate the tradeoffs in their design decisions.

System design at senior level is where many candidates lose the offer. The interviewer expects you to drive the conversation — they shouldn't have to prompt you for capacity estimates, database choice, or failure modes. A strong structure: spend 5 minutes clarifying functional and non-functional requirements (read/write ratio, latency SLA, data volume, consistency needs). Then 5 minutes on capacity estimation (QPS, storage growth, bandwidth). Then sketch the high-level architecture (5-7 boxes max, don't over-draw). Then go deep on the 2-3 components most relevant to the problem. End with discussions of failure modes, monitoring, and how the system evolves at 10x scale. Avoid: jumping straight to drawing without requirements, forgetting to estimate, ignoring failure cases, and using buzzwords without explaining tradeoffs.

The behavioral round at senior level focuses on scope, influence, and ambiguity. Expect questions like: tell me about a time you led a project across multiple teams; describe a technical decision that was reversed and what you learned; tell me about a time you disagreed with your manager; how do you handle ambiguous requirements; describe your most complex debugging experience. Use the STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result) but emphasize the 'Action' part — interviewers want to hear your specific role, not what 'we' did. Prepare 8-10 stories that cover: technical leadership, conflict, failure, mentorship, ambiguity, scope expansion, technical depth, and customer impact. The same story can answer multiple questions if framed correctly.
